_note: Known-good example output from packet-to-analysis.md. Use for testing prompt output shape and brief template rendering. Property: The Beekman, A Thompson Hotel — New York City. Data is illustrative, not from a real scrape.

representative_quotes: The nine-story atrium is one of the most stunning hotel spaces I've ever been in.
Walking through the lobby felt like stepping into a different century. Genuinely jaw-dropping.
The building itself is the experience. I kept finding new details every time I walked through.
Concierge got us a last-minute reservation at a place we'd given up on. Didn't even hesitate.
Every staff member we encountered was warm without being performative about it.
The front desk remembered our names by the second morning. Rare at a hotel this size.
The cast iron details and original windows made it feel like a real New York room, not a hotel room.
Bed was exceptional. Linens, mattress, the works.
Our room was small but every inch felt intentional. Nothing felt cheap.
Steps from the Brooklyn Bridge and City Hall park. Couldn't have been more convenient.
The Financial District has gotten genuinely interesting — great base for exploring downtown.
Best hotel bar I've been to in New York. Not even close.
The drinks program feels like it belongs in a destination bar, not just a hotel amenity.
Breakfast was the one disappointment. Inconsistent quality for the price point.
Eggs were clearly sitting under a lamp. At these rates, that's not acceptable.
The breakfast menu hasn't changed in a year and feels like an afterthought.
top_complaints.internal_note: Complaints cluster around weekend brunch service specifically — possible staffing issue. Weekday reviews are softer on this. Worth flagging to operator before making sweeping breakfast recommendations.

representative_quotes: The room was beautiful but genuinely small. I knew going in, but still.
For what we paid, I expected more than 220 square feet.
The design makes the most of limited space, but it's still limited.
top_complaints.internal_note: This is a structural property issue, not operational. Website does disclose room sizes on some listings but not all. Primarily surfaces on 4-star reviews, not 1–2 stars — guests still satisfied overall.

top_complaints.theme: Noise between rooms and from street
representative_quotes: Could hear conversations from the adjacent room clearly. Historic building, not much insulation.
Beekman Street gets noisy on weekends. Worth requesting a courtyard-facing room.
Woke up twice Saturday night from street noise. Earplugs would have helped.
top_complaints.internal_note: Historic building insulation limits are real. Complaints are lower-severity but consistent. A proactive room tip in confirmation emails could deflect this.

top_complaints.theme: Valet parking cost and wait times
representative_quotes: $85/night for valet is steep even by NYC standards.
45-minute wait for car retrieval on checkout morning.
top_complaints.internal_note: NYC pricing context makes this understandable but the wait times are a legitimate operational flag. Low reputational impact because most guests arrive by transit.

representative_quotes: Gym is fine but doesn't match the overall quality of the property.
Limited cardio equipment — treadmill wait at 7am.
top_complaints.internal_note: Isolated across review window. Not a pattern. Include in internal brief for completeness but low priority for client.

website_alignment_gaps.website_claim: Positioned as a full-service luxury hotel with premium dining
website_alignment_gaps.guest_reality: Breakfast quality consistently underperforms relative to room rates and overall property quality
website_alignment_gaps.recommendation: Either elevate breakfast service quality to match positioning or soften dining claims on booking pages. Consider adding honest framing ('intimate, locally-sourced menu') rather than broad 'premium dining' language.

website_alignment_gaps.website_claim: Room descriptions emphasize spaciousness and comfort
website_alignment_gaps.guest_reality: Multiple guests surprised by room dimensions despite booking at rates implying larger spaces
website_alignment_gaps.recommendation: Surface square footage prominently in room descriptions. Guests who know going in rate this lower-concern; guests who feel surprised rate it higher-impact. Accurate expectation-setting protects reviews.

website_alignment_gaps.website_claim: Temple Court Bar described as a hotel amenity
website_alignment_gaps.guest_reality: Guests consistently experience it as a destination in its own right and recommend it independently
website_alignment_gaps.recommendation: Promote Temple Court Bar more aggressively as a standalone draw. It is converting non-hotel guests and generating positive reviews — underutilized as a marketing asset.

service_risk_areas.notes: Complaints cluster on Saturday and Sunday. Likely a staffing volume or kitchen prep issue on peak mornings. 22 total breakfast mentions, majority weekend-flagged.
9 mentions over 6 months. Not high-frequency but consistent. Checkout morning bottleneck appears to be the primary trigger.
Historic building insulation is structural. Proactive guest communication (courtyard room request tip, earplugs in-room) could deflect without operational cost.

guest_expectation_mismatches.expectation: Full-size luxury hotel room at a premium NYC rate
guest_expectation_mismatches.reality: Historic building constraints mean rooms run 180–280 sq ft in many categories

guest_expectation_mismatches.expectation: Premium breakfast dining included or of equivalent quality to room experience
guest_expectation_mismatches.reality: Breakfast is inconsistent and perceived as underperforming relative to overall property standard

quick_wins.action: Add room size (sq ft) prominently to all room booking descriptions
Send pre-arrival tip in confirmation email: 'For a quieter stay, request a courtyard-facing room when you arrive'
Feature Temple Court Bar separately in homepage and social — position as a destination, not just a hotel bar
Audit and update breakfast menu and weekend staffing coverage
Add a review response template addressing room size transparency — acknowledge it proactively and reframe around design intentionality

strategic_recommendations.recommendation: Develop a weekend F&B excellence standard separate from weekday operations
strategic_recommendations.rationale: Breakfast complaints cluster on weekends and represent the single highest-impact service gap relative to overall property quality and price tier. Closing this gap would meaningfully protect the 4.4 average rating.

strategic_recommendations.recommendation: Reposition Temple Court Bar as a standalone NYC destination in all marketing
strategic_recommendations.rationale: 27 unprompted positive mentions across a 6-month window. Guests already treat it as a destination — the marketing hasn't caught up. Stronger positioning increases non-guest foot traffic and generates organic review volume.

strategic_recommendations.recommendation: Audit all OTA and direct booking room listings for size transparency
strategic_recommendations.rationale: Room size is the second most common friction theme and nearly always comes from surprised guests rather than guests who knew going in. Expectation alignment is a direct review score protector at this price tier.

strategic_recommendations.recommendation: Build a proactive pre-arrival communication sequence addressing the top known friction points
strategic_recommendations.rationale: Noise concerns and room size surprises are structurally fixed. Guest education (courtyard rooms, what to expect from a historic building) can deflect 30–40% of those complaints at near-zero operational cost.

reputation_risk_score.summary: The Beekman holds a strong overall reputation driven by its architecture, staff, and bar program. The primary risk is the breakfast quality gap — it is the only recurring operational complaint that punches above its frequency weight given the price tier. Room size perceptions are structural and partially manageable through expectation-setting. No critical reputation threats present in this review window. Trajectory is stable but breakfast service is a clear ceiling on reaching 4.6+ average.

confidence_notes.note: 14 reviews excluded for low confidence — predominantly fragmented captures from TripAdvisor mobile listings where review text was <15 words. Exclusion does not materially affect pattern findings.
9 duplicates excluded — same review text appeared on both Google and Booking.com for 9 entries. Deduplicated by text similarity. Source attribution preserved in packet.

Breakfast complaint pattern is strong in frequency but the weekend-vs-weekday clustering is inferred from date metadata, not from explicit reviewer statements in all cases. Treat weekend attribution as probable, not confirmed.
Gym complaint (7 mentions) treated as isolated/not a pattern. If review volume increases and gym mentions rise above 12, reclassify as pattern.

guest_language_bank.quote: One of the most stunning hotel spaces I've ever been in.
The building itself is the experience.
Best hotel bar I've been to in New York. Not even close.
Warm without being performative about it.
The drinks program feels like it belongs in a destination bar.
